safe_builtins = dict((key, value) for key, value in vars(__builtins__).items() 
    if not (key.startswith('__') and key.endswith('__')) and

```
and then
```
satisfiable = bool(eval(test, {'__builtins__': safe_builtins}, {}))

> I am not sure that the library can provide a default that works for all services (however we could allow it to be customised or enable/disable retrying)

Right now, the best way to customize things is to vendor the whole chunk of code. For example, `HTTPSMultiRepositoryPublicKeyRetriever` hardcodes `HTTPSPublicKeyRetriever` with just one init parameter, so attempting to customize one can break the other.

I'd like to see a more flexible implementation, but I didn't want to refactor everything just to get retries. ¯\\\_(ツ)_/¯

> If we retry by default are we preventing issues from being detected?

Only temporary issues, and only on the requesting side.

> How often are you seeing connections fail that work upon retrying them?

Transient connection are currently the primary server error that shows up in the logs. Cleaning them up will increase the signal to noise ratio.

This is definitely wanted by the byte server so if not in this PR, then the next one.  Arguably, It makes the protocol cleaner as well.  Until fairly recently, msgid was always an integer. Then, when adding multiple simultaneous loads and prefetch, I added the hack of letting msgid be an (oid,tid) tuple. This hack worked because of the dynamic nature of Python. Rust, of course, is statically typed and it's a lot more convenient for msgid to always be an int. 

IIRC it *was* also helpful for msgpack, because by default, "arrays" are decoded as lists, which was messing up msgid lookup. I think I later change the default decoding for arrays to tuple, which would have made this a non-issue.

The corresponding line in SBProfile is the important one.  That's the constructor that builds the base SBProfile object with whatever the appropriate pimpl type is.  Something derived from SBProfileImpl, such as SBInterpolatedImageImpl.  This is where all the magic is that makes SBProfiles act like python objects, being trivially copyable and such.

It's a breaking change for a different reason than the ones you provide, though.

(1 & 2).  Actually, the `fields` argument can still be used as a positional parameter, identically how `properties` argument was before. It remains a a [positional-or-keyword parameter](https://docs.python.org/2/glossary.html#term-parameter) after adding a default value. If they used the `properties` argument positionally it works the same and if they used it as a keyword argument it works the same.

(3). The actual breaking change here is with the retry argument. Someone could have used the `retry` argument positionally, which now would get passed to `properties` instead. If we kept with the strategy of deprecating `properties`, I should have added it as a keyword argument to the end or have used `**kwargs` to fetch it.

this is slightly confusing. In terms of Go best practices, the `cmd` package should be a "dumb" implementation of each command, only being given an argument list which then it can pass along to the underlying client libraries. For example, in golang.org/src/cmd/go/build.go, they use a [Command structure](http://golang.org/src/cmd/go/build.go#L30), which then calls [rubBuild](http://golang.org/src/cmd/go/build.go#L261) that takes an argument list. This is also replicated amongst other projects in the community. I think it'd be better to standardize and follow the same best practices as other projects. That way each command can specify its own flagset that is independent of the root binary's flagset, among other benefits (standardization, ability for users to call cmd.Register with an argument list without it breaking across versions etc.). What do you think about taking this approach?

It is a hard decision, and I am in an open mind about this. There are three ways for this situation:
1. When no Content-Type is provided, assume it is a utf-8 string
2. When no Content-Type is provided, always keep it as bytes
3. When no Content-Type is provided, first try to parse it as an utf-8 string (with "strict"), and when exception occurs, return the raw bytes

Each has their own advantages and disadvantages. I'm looking at the code which is processing application/x-www-form-urlencoded data and it is:
```python
            data = yield from self.read()
            if data:
                charset = self.charset or 'utf-8'
                out.extend(
                    parse_qsl(
                        data.rstrip().decode(charset),
                        encoding=charset))
```
Notice that this piece of code assume charset to be utf-8 when no charset is provided through Content-Type header (notice that a %NN encoded character is really a byte). It always decode data into string. So I suggest use the same strategy for multipart/form-data format.

As I have said, a lot of browsers do not send Content-Type header for sub parts of the form data - in most times, they are indeed encoded into utf-8. There is nothing a developer can do about this. If multipart/form-data post data is parsed into bytes, a developer is forced to check the data type of post() every time if he wants to accept both format. To decide to not decode a bytes object is easy, but the user may be suprised to see that the return type for multipart/form-data and application/x-www-from-urlencoded is so different. And he would also have a hard time when some tools or browsers actually provide the Content-Type header.

After we have a conclusion maybe we should add it into the document.

@vfdev-5 yes you're right! it should be simplified. 

I do so simply because in **functional**, if you give a 2-element tuple (a, b) as **padding**, it will interpret it as (width, height). 

this is not natural because if you specify RandomCrop(size=(128, 32), padding=(16, 4)), you are probably expecting the height 128 padded with 16 and width 32 padded with 4. but in fact, it is inverse. So this patch of codes are intended for exchanging the positions of these two elements, although it looks, yeah, a little messy ..
